ModuleName: 2 - Ethics, Data Types, and Data Sources in Analytics

Artifacts.Name: Module Topics
Artifacts.Description: A structured outline of the module's learning topics, organized into sections covering ethical foundations, privacy and data protection, and bias and fairness, which instructors can use to plan lessons and learners can use to navigate the module's key concepts.

Artifacts.Name: Module Topics (HTML)
Artifacts.Description: A navigation page listing the module's topics—ethical foundations, fairness, transparency, and accountability in data-driven decision-making—that learners use to orient themselves and access the module's individual lessons.

Artifacts.Name: Ethics_Data_Types_generated.pptx
Artifacts.Description: A slide deck introducing ethics, data types, and data sources in analytics—covering moral frameworks, fairness, transparency, and bias—which instructors can present in class or share with learners as a visual study reference.

Artifacts.Name: Ethical Foundations in Data Analytics
Artifacts.Description: This reading introduces the ethical dimensions of data analytics — including autonomy, fairness, transparency, and accountability — providing learners with a conceptual framework for evaluating the moral implications of analytical work that affects real-world decisions.

Artifacts.Name: Privacy and Data Protection
Artifacts.Description: This reading covers the principles and legal frameworks governing privacy and data protection in analytics, explaining how analysts should handle personal information responsibly to avoid harm and comply with regulations.

Artifacts.Name: Bias and Fairness in Data Analytics
Artifacts.Description: This reading examines how bias enters data analytics pipelines and the various definitions and approaches to fairness, helping learners identify and mitigate discriminatory outcomes in data-driven decision-making.

Artifacts.Name: Responsible and Ethical Use of Data
Artifacts.Description: This reading outlines the core responsibilities of data professionals beyond technical skill — including honest communication, ethical data use, and accountability — giving learners a framework for conducting analytics with integrity.

Artifacts.Name: Structured and Unstructured Data Types
Artifacts.Description: This reading distinguishes between structured and unstructured data types, explaining their characteristics, storage requirements, and implications for processing and analysis so learners can identify what kind of data they are working with.

Artifacts.Name: Common Data Sources and Repositories
Artifacts.Description: This reading surveys the landscape of data sources and repositories — including government, commercial, and open datasets — teaching learners how to locate, evaluate, and select appropriate data sources for an analytics project.

Artifacts.Name: Data Collection Methods and Web APIs
Artifacts.Description: This reading explains the methods used to collect data for analytics projects, with a focus on web APIs, covering how to retrieve data from live services and other sources so learners can acquire raw data programmatically.

Artifacts.Name: Data Collection Methods and Web APIs
Artifacts.Description: This reading explains the methods used to collect data for analytics projects, with a focus on web APIs, covering how to retrieve data from live services and other sources so learners can acquire raw data programmatically.

Artifacts.Name: Quiz Question Bank (20 questions)
Artifacts.Description: A 20-question quiz bank assessing learners' understanding of core module concepts such as why ethics matters in analytics, how algorithmic bias operates, and the importance of transparency in automated decision-making, which instructors can draw from to build graded assessments.

Artifacts.Name: Data on Trial: Evaluating Data Sources for Quality, Reliability, and Appropriateness
Artifacts.Description: A presentation and structured debate assignment in which learners evaluate real data sources against criteria for quality, reliability, and appropriateness using the TILT framework, preparing them to defend or challenge data source choices as they would in professional analytics practice.
